data.title: VOOV: Not Optimal, But A Solid Choice For Large-Cap Value ETF Investors
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Vanguard S&P 500 Value Index Fund ETF (VOOV) holds a diversified group of large-cap stocks with attractive value characteristics. However, one often-forgotten feature is that it also serves as an effective anti-momentum play in the early months following its Index's annual reconstitution in December. That's because its Index intentionally selects stocks that performed relatively poorly over the last twelve months, and this year, some growth stocks like Apple Inc. (AAPL) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) qualified. As a result, VOOV is more balanced than usual, and given its low portfolio beta, reasonable sector mix, and the aggressive earnings growth projections for most market leaders, I think there's a solid chance VOOV will end its three-year streak and outperform VOOG in 2026.

Nevertheless, since VOOV is designed to deliver consistent second- or third-quartile results in its category, investors should limit their expectations. For better potential results this year, I will profile two other large-cap value ETFs that look stronger fundamentally than VOOV right now, and use them to justify my neutral "hold" rating on the fund. I hope you enjoy the read, and as always, I look forward to answering any questions you might have in the comments section afterward.

VOOV Overview

Strategy Discussion: An Anti-Momentum Play

According to its website, VOOV tracks the S&P 500 Value Index, which "tracks the value companies of the S&P 500 Index as identified by three factors: book/price ratio, earnings/price ratio, and sales/price ratio." However, the Index methodology reveals how that's only partially true. When creating S&P Style Indexes, all S&P 500 companies receive both a value and a growth score, and the S&P categorizes stocks based on both factors. Categorizing stocks is sometimes straightforward, but S&P's method complicates it by introducing a one-year price momentum screen into the mix, as follows:

The momentum factor was incredibly strong last year, meaning top-performing growth stocks like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) easily qualified for the growth basket. Meanwhile, other growth stocks with less-impressive returns, including Apple, Amazon, and even Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), are top holdings in VOOV this year. It's a fascinating setup that positions VOOV as an anti-momentum play just as much as it is a value play for 2026.

Other fund characteristics for VOOV include a 0.07% expense ratio, a 0.04% 30-day median bid/ask spread, $6.3 billion in assets under management, and a 1.73% trailing twelve-month distribution rate, which makes income secondary to capital appreciation in terms of investment objectives.

VOOV Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

VOOV is better balanced than VOOG from a sector allocation perspective. As shown below, its largest two sectors (Technology and Financials) account for only 34% of the portfolio compared to 65% for VOOG's two largest sectors (Technology and Communication Services). Furthermore, VOOV has at least 3% allocated to all 11 GICS sectors, which supports the "consistent performer" label I've assigned it. Put differently, for VOOV to perform really badly, all segments of the market would have to sell off broadly at the same time, which I think is unlikely. Passive long-term investors will appreciate this feature, but it probably won't attract investors seeking to outperform the value market.

Instead, I'd like to highlight two other large-cap value ETFs I feel are better positioned than VOOV: the SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value Factor ETF (SEIV) and the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV). These ETFs have more tech sector exposure, but from a value factor perspective, I'll demonstrate shortly how they're just as competitive, if not better, than VOOV.

VOOV Performance Summary

Since October 2010, the first full month following its inception, VOOV has delivered an annualized 11.76% return compared to 15.88% for VOOG. This significant underperformance underscores how well growth stocks have performed over the last 10-15 years, but as I indicated earlier, the momentum factor also has a lot to do with it. For VOOV to be successful relative to VOOG, we're going to have to see that factor's strength normalize a bit because it's powered VOOG to 29.96%, 35.89%, and 22.11% gains in 2023, 2024, and 2025, respectively. A quick glance at the calendar-year returns table shows how that's not typical.

It's also rare for either ETF to outperform the other for four consecutive years, but beyond that, VOOV still offers some decent downside protection. As shown in the performance statistics table, its maximum drawdown was better (25.29% vs. 30.47%), and its "worst 12 months" return was a loss of 12.14% compared to 29.48% for VOOG. Total risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were still lower over this period, but a repeat of 2022, where high P/E ETFs like VOOG struggled as earnings expectations decelerated, would make VOOV look more competitive over the long run.

That said, there are plenty of choices in the large-cap value category, and I believe SEIV and FDVV are better positioned. Their track records are shorter, but since October 2026, FDVV has had the edge over VOOV on annualized returns (12.66% vs. 11.36%) and risk-adjusted returns despite steeper drawdowns.

And since June 2022, SEIV has outperformed both VOOV and FDVV. I've been impressed with this relatively new actively managed fund since my initial review in November 2025, and one differentiating feature is that it doesn't ignore the momentum factor. SEIV still trades very cheaply, but based on my conversation with its lead portfolio manager, they're very hesitant to select stocks that are trading cheaply for good reasons. That approach to value investing aligns with mine, and it's an example of how active managers can add value over passive funds.

VOOV Fundamental Analysis

Currently, VOOV trades with a 23.27x trailing P/E and 18.93x forward P/E, both of which represent substantial discounts to VOOG (30.59x and 24.27x) but nevertheless appear slightly elevated. As shown below, that's mainly sourced to its top ten holdings, Apple and Amazon. Also in the mix is Tesla, which qualified mainly because of its -32.59% 3Y EPS CAGR, one of the three value screens used in the selection process. Tesla's P/E ratios remain sky high, but it's a good example of how it's not always easy to classify a stock as "value" or "growth." Tesla actually has a 2.39% allocation in VOOG as well, and the two ETFs currently overlap by 25.51%, per my overlap tool.

The table above highlights VOOV's 0.92 five-year portfolio beta, which is the lowest in this peer group and supports its defensive nature. EBIT margins (21.08%), net margins (15.99%), and the portfolio's weighted average return on total capital (15.99%) are also solid, but the fact is that the market's highest-quality stocks are on the growth side with VOOG. Its margin and capital efficiency ratios are much better, so even though it may be susceptible to larger single-year drawdowns, the odds of a strong recovery are solid, which ultimately makes it a better long-term investment for those willing to tolerate the added risk.

In my view, SEIV addresses that risk quite well. Its 0.99 five-year portfolio beta and quality features are reasonably competitive with VOOV's, but its earnings growth rates (historical and estimated) are 2-4 points better, and its P/E ratios are substantially better (15.40x trailing and 11.31x forward). SEIV managers don't ignore the momentum factor either—its components are up 34.41% over the last year compared to only 13.15% for VOOV, and arguably, it's well-deserved. These same components reported earnings that were 7.36% above analyst expectations last quarter, which ranks #8/148 in the large-cap value category.

FDVV is also a solid alternative to VOOV. Following what I term a "barbell" approach to dividend investing, FDVV holds a balanced mix of value and growth stocks and is designed to perform well in various market environments. You'll notice that its margins, capital efficiency ratios, EPS growth rates, and P/E ratios are all better than VOOV's, plus it offers a substantially higher dividend yield. The table highlights how, at current prices, FDVV's holdings yield 3.14% on a weighted average basis, which should net shareholders 2.99% after adjusting out its 0.15% expense ratio. Meanwhile, VOOV nets only 1.80% after fees, so besides better company-level diversification, it really doesn't offer investors any meaningful advantage.

So Why Buy VOOV?

VOOV isn't my preferred pick, but it still does a good job delivering consistent returns year after year. To illustrate, consider the following table of annual returns for the 26 large-cap value ETFs that have traded since 2011.

By year, VOOV ranked as follows:

  • 2011: #22/26 (Quartile 4)
  • 2012: #7/26 (Quartile 2)
  • 2013: #9/26 (Quartile 2)
  • 2014: #21/26 (Quartile 4)
  • 2015: #16/26 (Quartile 3)
  • 2016: #14/26 (Quartile 3)
  • 2017: #15/26 (Quartile 3)
  • 2018: #20/26 (Quartile 4)
  • 2019: #1/26 (Quartile 1)
  • 2020: #16/26 (Quartile 3)
  • 2021: #18/26 (Quartile 3)
  • 2022: #16/26 (Quartile 3)
  • 2023: #3/26 (Quartile 1)
  • 2024: #21/26 (Quartile 4)
  • 2025: #19/26 (Quartile 3)

VOOV ranks in the second or third quartiles most of the time, and even in years when it ranks in the fourth quartile (2011, 2014, 2018, and 2024), performance isn't right at the bottom of its category. While this might not seem impressive at first, avoiding big losses has proven crucial to VOOV's success, since over this 15-year period, VOOV's 11.35% annualized return ranks #10/26 overall. Therefore, while it may be a boring choice, slightly outperforming its peers over time is exactly what VOOV is designed to do.

Investment Recommendation

VOOV is a passive large-cap ETF offered by Vanguard that holds stocks with strong value and weak growth characteristics. Its Index also ranks S&P 500 stocks based on their one-year price returns, so VOOV also holds several growth stocks, like Apple and Amazon, that underperformed in 2025. In this sense, it's an anti-momentum play that could lead to solid returns relative to VOOG if the momentum factor loses some of its strength this year. Investors will also appreciate that VOOV is a consistent performer, and avoiding big losses is what's made it a second-quartile large-cap value ETF over the last 15 years.

However, for those willing to accept more risk, I don't think it's the optimal choice. This article identified SEIV and FDVV as two peers that do a better job balancing value, growth, quality, momentum, and dividends. While they're less diversified than VOOV, that's likely not an issue for investors holding multiple ETFs, and I expect they will continue to outperform VOOV moving forward. As a result, a "hold" rating is most appropriate. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your questions and comments below.

data.title: Armata Pharmaceuticals: Intriguing Antibiotic Adjunct, But Too Many Potential Red Flags
data.description:
Investment Overview

Armata Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ARMP) stock began trading on the New York Stock Exchange in May 2019 as a result of a merger between C3J Therapeutics and AmpliPhi Biosciences Corporation. At the time, Armata discussed its business as follows:

Armata is a clinical-stage biotechnology company focused on the development of precisely targeted bacteriophage therapeutics for antibiotic-resistant infections using its proprietary bacteriophage-based technology.

Armata's lead product candidate, AP-SA01, targets Staphylococcus aureus including multidrug-resistant strains. The Company is also developing and advancing a broad pipeline of synthetic phage candidates, including a synthetic phage for Pseudomonas aeruginosa, leveraging its proprietary phage-specific GMP manufacturing capabilities.

According to Armata's 2024 annual report/10-K submission, in a study in the US:

A total of 18 patients with serious or life-threatening S. aureus infections (including bacteremia, endocarditis, ventricular-assist device infection, prosthetic joint infection) not responding to standard-of-care antibiotic therapy were treated with AP-SA01.

AP-SA01 was administered intravenously, with most patients treated for 14 days, every 12 hours as an adjunct to antibiotic therapy. Investigators concluded that intravenous administration of AP-SA01 was well-tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events.

Today, however, a next-generation version of the candidate, AP-SA02, is the lead clinical program in Armata's pipeline, as shown in the slide below, taken from a company corporate presentation:

Armata stock experienced a series of peaks and troughs in its early years, reaching highs of ~$5.5 per share and lows of ~$1.5, with no major pipeline progress to report, until October last year, when shares doubled in value, rising from ~$3.5 to >$7, as the company shared data from its Phase 2a study of AP-SA02 in patients with complicated S. aureus.

According to a press release:

The Phase 2a study enrolled and dosed 42 patients, with 29 randomized to AP-SA02 in addition to BAT (best available therapy) and 13 to placebo (BAT alone). Methicillin-resistant S. aureus ("MRSA") was the causative pathogen in ~38% of both the AP-SA02 and placebo groups.

Day 12 clinical response rates were higher in the AP-SA02 group — 88% (21/24) versus 58% (7/12) in the placebo group as assessed by blinded site investigators ("PI") (p = 0.047), and 83% (20/24) in the AP-SA02 group versus 58% (7/12) in the placebo group as assessed by the blinded Adjudication Committee ("AC").

Patients treated with AP-SA02 showed trends toward rapid normalization of C-reactive protein, shorter time to negative blood culture, quicker time to resolution of signs and symptoms at the infection site, shorter intensive care unit and hospital utilization.

AP-SA02 was well-tolerated with no serious adverse events related to the study drug.

Since this positive data was reported, Armata stock has kept rising, trading at a value of >$11 per share today, which values the company at a market cap of $405m (at the time of writing).

This is in part due to a press release dated January 13th, 2026, in which the company states that it had concluded an end-of-phase 2 ("EOPT") study with the FDA and planned to initiate a Phase 3 study of AP-SA02 in complicated S. aureus bacteremia, which it anticipated beginning in the second half of this year.

AP-SA02 + Standard-Of-Care: The New Standard-Of-Care? A Successful Phase 3 Is Still Required

It's important to note that AP-SA02 works in combination with a standard-of-care ("SoC") antibiotic, with the ability to potentially improve outcomes for patients suffering from complicated staphylococcus aureus bacteremia ("SAB"), as explained in the below slide:

The Phase 2a study demographics are shown below:

Antibiotics used are shown below:

And clinical outcomes are shown below:

Besides these apparently impressive results, AP-SA02 also performed significantly better in terms of mean time to hospital discharge (11.7 days versus 19.2 days), time to initial SAB resolution (2.7 days versus 9.3 days), and C-reactive protein ("CRP") test scores (50.2 vs 97.3) - CRP is released into the bloodstream when the immune system detects inflammation.

Safety-wise, results between the AP-SA02 +BAY were broadly comparable to placeARbo/BAT only, with 4 serious adverse events out of 29 patients for the former and 3 out of 13 patients for the latter. There was a single study drug-related treatment adverse event in the study arm, however, and none in the placebo arm.

Armata says that:

Defined phage variants in the AP-SA02 drug product ensure an intrinsic adaptive mechanism — a flexibility that may be key to achieving effective phage therapy from patient to patient.

The proposed Phase 3 study will have the same endpoint as the Phase 2a, being clinical response at the Test of Cure (TOC) visit at 7 or 28 days ("clinical response is resolution of all signs and symptoms associated with the bacteremia and negative blood culture"), with secondary endpoints including clinical response at Day 14, time to hospital discharge, and all-cause mortality.

450 patients is the target enrollment, with safety data from 300 apparently being adequate to support a Biologics License Application ("BLA") submission to the FDA, requesting commercial approval.

Operationally Ready, But Is Market Opportunity Sufficient

In February this year, Armata announced that AP-SA02 had received a Qualified Infectious Disease Product ("QIDP") for adjunct treatment of complicated bacteremia caused by methicillin-sensitive S. aureus ("MSSA") or methicillin-resistant S. aureus ("MRSA") from the FDA. According to Armata:

To achieve QIDP designation, a drug candidate must be intended to treat serious or life-threatening infections, particularly those caused by bacteria and fungi that are resistant to treatment, or that treat qualifying resistant pathogens identified by the FDA.

It's another positive step forward for Armata, who also shared in its Q3 2025 earnings press release that its:

state-of-the-art current Good Manufacturing Practice ("cGMP") manufacturing facility in Los Angeles, California, has been formally commissioned.

  • Armata's 56,000 square foot facility includes 10,000 square feet of cGMP clean rooms, an automated fill and finish suite, and quality control laboratories, to support future clinical trials and full commercialization as well as potential partnering and contract manufacturing opportunities.

At the same time, Armata reported a cash position of $14.8m, total current liabilities of $140m, including a convertible loan of $48m, and $83m of "term debt". Net loss for the first 9 months of 2025 was also reported as $(49.5m). The company's accumulated deficit stood at $377m as of the end of Q3 2025.

During its lifetime, Armata has frequently secured funding from Innoviva Strategic Opportunities LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary of Innoviva (INVA), a company that makes investments in companies in exchange for future royalty streams. Innoviva is apparently Armata's principal stockholder - according to the company's 2024 annual report/10-K submission:

Innoviva, our principal stockholder, beneficially owns greater than 50% of our outstanding shares of Common Stock, which causes us to be deemed a “controlled company” under the rules of the New York Stock Exchange (the “NYSE”). In addition, Innoviva’s interests in our business may be different than our other stockholders.

Innoviva owns 69.3% of the Company’s outstanding equity as of December 31, 2024. The Company also received $90.0 million in total debt financing from Innoviva SO during 2023 and in March 2024.

In March and August 2025, Innoviva loaned Armata $10m and $15m at an interest rate of 14% (source: Q3 10-Q filing). It seems odd that a company would lend at such a high-interest rate to a company it has a ~69% stake in - I sense a potential red flag waving - but with that said, it also seems very clear Armata will require more funding to complete its Phase 3 study of AP-SA02 and to fund an approval submission and, potentially, a commercial launch.

From my experience covering biotechs involved in developing antibiotics, the market opportunity was often considered weak, as antibiotics do not command high list prices, and patients's ability to develop resistance to them means they have a short shelf life.

With AP-SA02 being an adjunct product, the cost of which would be added to that of the antibiotic itself, my feeling is that Phase 3 will need to establish a clear and obvious benefit in order to justify the additional cost - hospitals typically have stretched budgets, and insurers may balk at paying for two products when one will suffice.

Given the serious nature of SAB, however, if the pivotal study does succeed, my best guess would be that the FDA would move swiftly to approve the drug, as this is an area of unmet need and S. aureus infections can be deadly.

Some research online suggests the market opportunity for the antibiotics used in the Phase 2a study is ~$4bn - $5bn, although this should be considered a "ballpark" figure as genuine data appears to be elusive. My best guess would be that Armata could target a peak revenue opportunity of ~$250m - $500m.

Concluding Thoughts: Should You Consider Acquiring Armata Stock?

Clearly, Armata shareholders will be delighted with the progress made by the company in the past few months and by the returns shares have delivered - they are up >475% on a 1-year basis at the time of writing.

In terms of the opportunity that lies ahead, I'd be more circumspect. Based on the "don't invest in what you don't understand" thesis, I see several red flags, the main one being financing. Here is a statement from Armata's Q3 10-Q filing:

We have incurred net losses since our inception and have negative operating cash flows. Our cash and cash equivalents of $14.8 million as of September 30, 2025 will not be sufficient to fund our operations for the next 12 months from the date of issuance of our condensed consolidated financial statements for the nine months ended September 30, 2025.

We plan to control our expenses and to raise additional capital through a combination of public and private equity, debt financing, strategic alliances, and grant and award arrangements.

These circumstances raise substantial doubt about our ability to continue as a going concern.

The fact that Innoviva is a majority shareholder may mean the company will secure the financing it needs - why would Innoviva allow bankruptcy for a company it majority owns? But since I don't have any real insight or clarity around what future funding arrangements may look like, I'd advise investors to proceed with extreme caution.

While I'd say that AP-SA02 appears to be a strong product based on recent data readouts and ongoing support from the FDA, whether it will be a long-term commercially profitable asset for Armata is open to debate. Personally, as discussed, I'd estimate peak revenues, if approved, in the low triple-digit millions - will that be enough to turn Armata from a ~$400m market cap company today into a billion-dollar company, say? I can't state with real optimism that would be the case, as I suspect profit margins selling this drug would be thin to non-existent.

Finally, I'd make the point that the stated main aim of Armata's business is to develop bacteriophage therapeutics - as per the 10-Q filing:

We see bacteriophages as a potentially safer and effective alternative to antibiotics and an essential response to the growing bacterial resistance to current classes of antibiotics. Bacteriophages or “phages” have a powerful and highly differentiated mechanism of action that enables binding to and killing of specific targeted bacteria while uniquely preserving the normal human microbiome or “healthy bacteria”.

AP-PA02 is targeting cystic fibrosis ("CF") and non-CF bronchiectasis, and a Phase 1b/2a study was completed in 2023 in CF and in NCFB in 2024. Results appear to have lacked compelling signs of efficacy, and the product does not seem to garner a mention in Armata's Q3 earnings release, suggesting these programs may have been deprioritized, which is hardly surprising given current funding.

To conclude, then, I am personally impressed by the AP-SA02 results, and in that sense, recent gains seem justified, but I have to admit to being somewhat mystified about how Armata can conduct a successful pivotal study, complete a BLA, secure commercial approval, and launch this product based on an analysis of current funding.

Perhaps the government will step in and provide some funding - after all, given the company is bringing its manufacturing facility online, it must presumably be confident of raising further funding, and there is a potentially clear benefit to bringing this product to market (if P3 data echoes P2a data).

I suspect there is more volatility ahead for Armata stock, as there is a lot more that management may communicate in 2026 around funding, pivotal study progress, and route to market, but I also suspect that a downward trend in the stock price may be likelier as the market digests recent gains and awaits further news on study initiation.

A stock to monitor, then, with at least one program with genuinely intriguing potential but not one to invest in today, in my view at least.

data.title: Ollie's Bargain Outlet: Solid 2026 Outlook, Attractive Valuation
data.description:
In today's column we reiterate a buy call on Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings, Inc. (OLLI). We believe that the pullback of nearly 40 points from the highs has provided an entry opportunity over the last few months. We like it here from $105 down and still think adding legs in $7–$8 increments lower on broader market weakness is a good plan.

Why? The company continues to expand its store base, has really worked to maximize its purchase orders of overstock items and turn those into sales at strong margins to its growing customer base, and, of course, earnings growth. With consumers seemingly more and more price-conscious each year, this company is in a sweet spot to continue growth. So, as shares have retraced, and considering the just-reported performance and outlook for 2026, we think shares remain a Buy. Let us discuss.

Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings' Q4 2025 Sales And Earnings

We were a bit surprised that for Q4 OLLI missed on both the top and the bottom-line earnings figures. The misses were mild, but they were misses nonetheless, to be sure. Sales were up a strong 17% from last year, however, to $779 million but missed by $4.4 million against consensus estimates. The slight miss trickled down to the earnings line. The company reported adjusted net income that rose 16% from a year ago to $85.4 million. This is strong growth. On a per-share basis, it was a $0.02 miss against consensus but rose 17% from $1.39 as adjusted. We also like to look at the EBITDA figure, which was up 16% to $127 million from $109 million. Sales were helped by increasing comparable sales and more stores opening during the year. There were 645 stores at the end of Q4, versus 559 to start the year. Despite the miss, the quarter was good in our opinion. Let's talk comps.

Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings' Q4 2025 Comparable Sales

So, as noted, sales got a boost from more stores being opened. The proliferation of stores is another reason we like buying shares for investment. There is a lot of room for expansion. The number one metric we watch in retailers is the comparable sales figure. While the sales missed slightly, comps were positive. Folks, comparable store sales were up 3.6% from the prior year. And we want to also point out that back in Q4 2025, comps were a positive 2.8%. So, 80 basis points of expansion, and on a stacked basis, this is 6.4% comparable sales growth over two years. Not too bad at all. How about the margins on sales?

Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings' Q4 2025 Margins

We like to see margins at about 40% or higher here. That is a solid gross margin for a retailer, especially one that is selling overstock items and buying wholesale while still offering discounted prices. So we closely watch margins. Now, gross margin was down 80 basis points from a year ago, but this was expected. The 39.9% margin is actually better than management had expected. We like that SG&A expenses as a percentage of sales dropped 130 basis points to 24.2% versus last year. This was from strong merchandise margins, effective promotions, and lower opening costs for new stores. Further, operating income was higher by 24%, to $109 million.

Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings 2026 Outlook

The outlook is strong here, folks. We saw loyalty members hit 17 million at the end of the year while opening a record 86 stores. This bodes well. Given the performance, the company gave solid guidance. Eric van der Valk, President and Chief Executive Officer, stated:

We had a strong fourth quarter to cap off an exceptional year. In the fourth quarter, we delivered better than expected sales and earnings, driven by solid comp growth, healthy margins, and disciplined expense control.

As we look to the new fiscal year, we are looking at another year of strong growth. Another 75 stores are set to be open. We thought the comparable sales guide of up 2% was a bit conservative, but the margin expectation is for 40.5% margins, above our 40% target that we like to see. The company will buy back another $100 million in shares and sees sales of $3 billion, with EPS of $4.45. So, at $105, shares are trading at an FWD multiple of just 23.5X. This is basically a market multiple for EPS growth of 15% or more. That is attractive in our opinion.

OLLI Balance Sheet Is Strong

We just want to mention there is a fortress balance sheet here despite new store openings and share repurchases. The balance sheet shows cash and cash equivalents of $260 million, short-term investments of $36 million, and long-term investments of $266 million. Ollie's still has no borrowings outstanding under its $100 million revolving credit facility; long-term debt is a mere $0.97 million. This is a great balance sheet here.

Final Thoughts

The Q4 results were a bit rough on the top and bottom line, but it was a good quarter. Sure, we like to see beats, but the comparable sales figures were solid, as were margins. More importantly, the outlook is for solid 2026 growth, and we are trading at about a market multiple. We like buying OLLI at $105 and below.

data.title: Global Leading Indicators, February 2026 - Upturn Confirmed; Will It Slip In Rising Oil Prices?
data.description:
The February 2026 edition of the global LEI chartbook can be found here. Additional details on the methodology are available here.

The February update of the global LEI chartbook confirms that a broad-based upturn in global cyclical activity has been underway since the end of the third quarter last year. Granted, the number of LEIs currently in expansion - 16 - is slightly lower than at previous cyclical peaks. However, the February update and revisions point to a stabilising expansion at this rate, which remains robust overall.

The big question now is whether the upturn will falter in the face of the energy price shock ignited by the war in Iran.

Time will tell.

In many ways, the war in Iran - and how investors interpret it in relation to Mr. Trump’s motives and expectations of an endgame, or lack thereof - is symptomatic of how markets think about geopolitical risk in an era of volatile US foreign policy. Let’s give the devil his due. Almost everyone agrees that Iran cannot be allowed to develop nuclear weapons, and few people will shed many tears over the demise of Ayatollah Khamenei, let alone the regime that has sustained him. Mr. Trump, aided and abetted by Israel, has seized what he views as a unique opportunity to deliver a decisive blow to a belligerent Iranian regime that has long sown instability in an already volatile region. In this sense, Mr. Trump is sticking to his opportunistic reaction function - picking what he sees as low-hanging fruit.

So far, so good. The Iranian military cannot lay a glove on either US or Israeli forces, which now appears to be emboldening the White House to make an example of the country, I suspect for a reason that has yet to be fully recognised by many observers.

Mr. Trump may genuinely believe that a show of force in Iran plays well with the domestic MAGA crowd. Many commentators will rightly point to the significant risk he is taking. After all, he was elected on a platform of putting America first, defined at least in part as avoiding new wars in the Middle East without a clear objective or endgame. Yet, Mr. Trump is now betting that images of the US military striking Iran will resonate with swing voters in the upcoming midterm elections. Moreover, the easier it appears for the US to pound Iran militarily - and at the moment, it does look relatively easy - the lower the perceived risk that his political base will punish him for becoming entangled in a prolonged conflict.

The president is, as usual, engaged in a high-stakes game. Whatever the White House may believe about the MAGA-boosting credentials of a military show of force, the contradiction between the pledge to avoid “forever wars” in the Middle East and the possibility of entering one is difficult to ignore. This conflict could usher in an era of relative peace and stability in the Middle East - or the Iranian republic and its civil society could implode, with destabilising consequences for the broader region. It is not clear that the White House is overly concerned either way.

Then there is the surge in oil and gas prices, threatening a new inflation shock, higher interest rates, and tighter financial conditions. As impressive as it may be to watch footage of a US submarine sinking a hapless Iranian warship, it does little to help US consumers and businesses who may now be staring down the barrel of a good old-fashioned energy shock at a time when inflation is already elevated.

As I write this on Sunday, March 8, markets will primarily be watching for signs that a trifecta of rising energy prices, rising bond yields, and falling stock prices might eventually prompt Mr. Trump to back down. For now, signals from the White House remain hawkish, suggesting that markets may need to inflict more pain before forcing a so-called “TACO.” This, in turn, threatens a prolonged rise in energy prices and sustained market volatility that could spill over into real economic activity.

The unique challenge for the global economy at this juncture is that underlying inflation remains elevated following the post-COVID shock and the war in Ukraine. Soft landings - defined as the ability to raise interest rates enough to bring down inflation without triggering a recession - leave the economy vulnerable to renewed inflation shocks. Should that occur, central banks could once again be compelled by incoming data to tighten monetary policy in order to deliver a decisive blow to inflation, which many policymakers - perhaps influenced by recency bias - may feel was never fully defeated in the first place.

Such a scenario would risk ushering in a more traditional tightening cycle, in which the price of restoring price stability is a recession and a significant rise in unemployment.

  • Sixteen out of 20 leading indicators were trending upward in February, unchanged from September last year after revisions, which points to a sustained upturn in global economic activity since the beginning of the third quarter.

  • [Reproduced from the January update] So what happens next? The chart above suggests that growth in coincident indicators typically continues to accelerate for at least six to twelve months after a cyclical high in leading indicators, pointing to robust global cyclical activity through most of 2026. But what about markets? To explore this, I conducted a small study, sampling six-month returns on the S&P 500 ahead of instances where my LEI diffusion index reached 15 or higher and was rising on a six-month basis. This yielded 38 instances in a sample going back to 2003, with an average six-month return of 7.6%. The ratio of positive to negative returns is solid at 32:6. The maximum return was 27% - in the six months starting in October 2020 - while the worst was -7% in the six months beginning in 2009. In short, just because global LEIs are at their highs - where they typically do not remain for more than six to nine months - does not mean investors should sell equities in anticipation of a downturn.

  • Coincident indicators have settled into a solid expansion, consistent with the upturn in leading indicators. Growth in global industrial production and trade accelerated to 4.1% year over year in December, unchanged from an upwardly revised November headline. The strength in LEIs suggests that further acceleration is likely.

  • The three-year rolling Z-score of the global LEI - often a reliable early signal of turning points in the global cycle - is still rising, reaching 2.69 in January, an all-time high in this sample.

  • Equity market returns rebounded somewhat in February, rebounding from weakness at the start of the year, but now face stiff headwinds as global energy prices soar and volatility rises. Energy stocks and defensives are set to outperform as long as our eyes are peeled in Iran, but the trajectory of leading indicators suggests that underlying sector rotation models should continue to favour cyclical sectors more generally,

  • The first principal component (PC1) of global LEIs remained in a clear and strengthening downtrend in February. PC1 captures common cyclical patterns across countries and typically peaks during synchronized global downturns. Its current weakness suggests growing divergence among economies, which is generally associated with cyclical strength in the global economy.

  • Country-level data point to a broad and robust upturn in global economic activity midway through the first quarter of 2026. China’s LEI remains the only notable weak spot - significant given the size of its economy, which is also weighing on the LEI in Asia.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CIBR Provides Good Entry Point For Cybersecurity Growth
data.description:
First Trust Nasdaq Cybersecurity ETF (CIBR) delivered a 7% return since my previous coverage, as the cybersecurity sector is gaining importance. My previous article called for up to 15% upside, as cybersecurity providers could be less affected by tariff-induced macro uncertainty. However, the industry players have been affected by market correction in the related AI sector. But, going forward, I believe that cybersecurity is becoming a critical element of risk management amid mass digitalization and migration to the cloud and the widespread adoption of artificial intelligence technologies. The latter could support a robust long-term demand for cybersecurity services and products. And despite the recent pullback, I would keep my bullish thesis on the CIBR ETF. I believe that fundamental drivers for cybersecurity solutions' growth remain intact, providing an attractive entry point for up to 25% upside potential.

Fund Overview

First Trust Nasdaq Cybersecurity ETF is established to provide exposure to the cybersecurity segment of the technology and industrial sectors. The fund tracks and replicates the performance of the NASDAQ CEA Cybersecurity Index, charging the investors with a 0.58% cost ratio per annum.

The index employs a liquidity-weighted approach and selects positions that satisfy criteria for a market capitalization of $250+ million and a free float of 20%+. Since my last coverage, the fund grew its total assets under management to $9.93 billion, which is an increase of circa $2.8 billion, wherein net inflows to the fund amounted to $2.11 billion.

The ETF remained quite concentrated, where the top 15 holdings account for 81% of the portfolio (vs. 79% in the previous article). Looking at the core portfolio holding, the fund increased its weight in Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) to 9.1% (vs. 7.8%), where other top positions remained largely with the same weight as follows: Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) with 7.7% weight, CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) with 7.5% weight, Infosys Limited (INFY) with 7.3% weight, and Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) with 7.3% share.

Looking at the performance, the fund delivered a 7% total return since my previous article, which highlighted the need for improved protection amid GenAI's rapid development. However, the cybersecurity industry has exhibited a negative performance since the beginning of the year, falling below the broad market. CIBR is down 7.2% to date, while Amplify Cybersecurity ETF (HACK) and iShares Cybersecurity and Tech ETF (IHAK) are slightly better off.

However, the cybersecurity sector is showing structural growth driven mainly by the transition to the cloud environment and the adoption of AI-based solutions. For reference, the global spending on information security grew by 10% in 2025 to $213 billion and is expected to further increase by 12.5% in 2026. This indicates a sustainable growth in cybersecurity solution demand not just from the corporate budgets but from the public sectors as well. I expect the latter to be a powerful demand factor, especially when it comes to national security and defense matters, as the U.S. government is actively implementing the artificial intelligence tools from the main frontier model developers.

Still, the demand for cybersecurity solutions is driven primarily by the increasing number and complexity of attacks. And with the rapid proliferation of artificial intelligence technology, particularly agentic AI, investments in cybersecurity could be viewed as a critical part of the corporate risk management item. As a result, businesses can’t afford to cut security expenditures going forward. Last time I wrote that in light of the escalation of the trade war, cybersecurity could become the least susceptible area of the technology sector. Although the global trade conditions remain uncertain and macroeconomic and geopolitical concerns persist, I believe that cybersecurity solutions and services remain a critical investment vertical that corporations cannot afford to cut.

Investment Attractiveness

To estimate the fund’s future potential, I will resort to a weighted average of the target prices of the top 25 portfolio companies. The approach indicates a strong upside potential of 25% due to the upbeat analyst estimates regarding the cybersecurity players.

Looking at the table, a significant part of the fund’s growth potential, or approximately 12%, is concentrated in its top five holdings. In particular, Infosys could benefit from its comprehensive suite of digital platforms and solutions and the strategic focus on AI. Broadcom is among the most prominent semiconductor stocks, thanks to the rapid expansion of the data center infrastructure and growing interest in energy-efficient AI compute. Moreover, with the government requirement for data center operators to bring their own power supply, the demand for ASIC solutions design and adoption could accelerate significantly. Rubrik, Inc. (RBRK) could also profit from the AI capacity build-out thanks to its enterprise and cloud data protection solutions. The race to build superior artificial intelligence models intensifies among the frontier developers, which makes data a valuable asset. Going forward, Palo Alto is a dominant force in the sector, with management predicting double-digit growth for its next-gen security portfolio. In addition, the rapid integration of AI capabilities in consumer devices could benefit Gen Digital, Inc. (GEN), with its endpoint security protection solutions.

At the same time, the valuation of CIBR decreased noticeably since the beginning of the year, where the forward P/E fell by 12.4% to 19.6x. Moreover, the ratio is now 18% below the 3-year median of 24x and is prominently below the valuation of the broad Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF of 22.7x.

As a result, the CIBR will remain in my Buy recommendation list, as the fund has a solid upside potential over the next 12-month investment horizon. I believe that the First Trust Nasdaq Cybersecurity ETF is a good instrument to gain exposure to the cybersecurity industry. The industry could remain a high-growth investment story for years to come as digital threats multiply while artificial intelligence technology proliferates.

Risk Factors

The primary constraint for cybersecurity providers remains the potential tightening of enterprise IT budgets in light of an uncertain macroeconomic and geopolitical picture. In the event of a broad economic slowdown or continued global trade tensions, IT spending may be squeezed, which could negatively reflect on cybersecurity investments and projects. Moreover, the cybersecurity player could be affected by market volatility, especially during periods of AI bubble fears and the overall negative sentiment for the tech sector. In addition, portfolio concentration in leading companies remains among the key questions as well.
